methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $280M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Quantum Computing (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Quantum Machines is a Series C quantum computing company that has raised $280M to date. The valuation analysis draws on a comparative universe of 11 quantum computing peers: 3 public companies (IonQ, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Quantum) and 8 private firms including IonQ, Quantinuum, Rigetti, D-Wave, and Infleqtion.

Given the early-stage and heterogeneous nature of quantum computing valuations, only one method proved applicable. Stage-progression analysis uses the $280M total raised as a proxy baseline and applies a 1.7x step-up multiplier typical of Series C-to-next-stage advancement, yielding an implied valuation of $476M. A peer-median approach was not feasible because the narrow Quantum Computing peer set lacks sufficient same-stage or adjacent-stage comparables meeting minimum statistical thresholds—only one adjacent-stage datapoint was identified from the universe of 7 same-sector companies.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $405M to $547M, with a median point of $476M. This range reflects a ±15% confidence band around the single applicable method. The primary uncertainty stems from the lack of robust market comparables at Quantum Machines' specific stage and valuation tier. Resolution would require either comparable transaction data from similar-stage quantum hardware or software companies, or public market re-ratings of existing quantum peers that would anchor relative valuations more firmly.
